attributes.stem: A 28-year-old woman at 32 weeks gestation presents with worsening heartburn and regurgitation, particularly after meals and when lying down. She has no previous history of gastro-oesophageal reflux disease (GORD) before pregnancy.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following best describes the pathophysiological mechanism of gastro-oesophageal reflux disease in pregnancy?
options: Increased gastric acid production during pregnancy
Increased gastric emptying due to hormonal changes
Increased oesophageal mucosal sensitivity to acid
Reduced bicarbonate secretion in the oesophagus
attributes.explanation:
The primary pathophysiological mechanism of gastro-oesophageal reflux disease (GORD) in pregnancy is decreased lower oesophageal sphincter pressure. This is primarily due to the effects of progesterone, which relaxes smooth muscle throughout the body, including the lower oesophageal sphincter. Additionally, pregnancy causes decreased gastric peristalsis and delayed gastric emptying, which further contribute to reflux symptoms.

The incorrect options can be explained:

  • Increased gastric acid production during pregnancy - Gastric acid production is not typically increased during pregnancy; in fact, it may be decreased in some women
  • Increased oesophageal mucosal sensitivity to acid - While this can occur in some GORD patients, it is not a primary mechanism specific to pregnancy-related reflux
  • Reduced bicarbonate secretion in the oesophagus - This is not a significant factor in pregnancy-related GORD
  • Increased gastric emptying due to hormonal changes - Pregnancy actually causes delayed gastric emptying, not increased emptying

As pregnancy progresses, the enlarging uterus increases intra-abdominal pressure, which further exacerbates reflux symptoms, particularly in the third trimester. The reflux of acidic or alkaline gastric contents into the oesophagus causes inflammation of the oesophageal mucosa, leading to the symptoms of heartburn, regurgitation, and in some cases, reflux oesophagitis.
learningPoints: Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease (GORD) in pregnancy is primarily caused by decreased lower oesophageal sphincter pressure, decreased gastric peristalsis, and delayed gastric emptying.
The enlarging uterus in later pregnancy exacerbates GORD by increasing intra-abdominal pressure.
Reflux of acidic or alkaline gastric contents into the oesophagus causes inflammation of the oesophageal mucosa, leading to reflux oesophagitis.
references: Nelson-Piercy C. Handbook of obstetric medicine. 6th ed. Boca Raton: CRC Press; 2021.
